title: Philosophy: Thinkers & Schools
description: Major philosophers and philosophical schools of thought

cards.back: Greek philosopher known for the Socratic method of questioning

cards.front: Which philosopher was sentenced to death by drinking hemlock in Athens?

cards.back: Greek philosopher, student of Socrates, founded the Academy in Athens

cards.front: Which philosopher wrote The Republic and founded the Academy?

cards.back: Greek philosopher, student of Plato, tutored Alexander the Great, wrote on logic, ethics, and science

cards.front: Which philosopher tutored Alexander the Great and wrote Nicomachean Ethics?

cards.back: German philosopher who wrote the Critique of Pure Reason and developed the categorical imperative

cards.front: Which philosopher developed the categorical imperative as a test for moral actions?

cards.back: French philosopher and mathematician known for "I think, therefore I am" (Cogito, ergo sum)

cards.front: Which philosopher said "I think, therefore I am"?

cards.back: German philosopher who wrote Thus Spoke Zarathustra and declared "God is dead"

cards.front: Which philosopher wrote Thus Spoke Zarathustra and coined the concept of the Übermensch?

cards.back: English philosopher who argued the mind is a tabula rasa (blank slate) and championed natural rights

cards.front: Which philosopher described the mind as a tabula rasa (blank slate)?

cards.back: English philosopher who wrote Leviathan and described life without government as "solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short"

cards.front: Which philosopher wrote Leviathan and argued for a strong central authority?

cards.back: Genevan philosopher who wrote The Social Contract and argued people are naturally good but corrupted by society

cards.front: Which philosopher wrote The Social Contract and believed humans are born free but "everywhere in chains"?

cards.back: French Enlightenment writer and philosopher who championed freedom of speech, religion, and separation of church and state

cards.front: Which Enlightenment philosopher wrote Candide and championed freedom of speech?

cards.back: Chinese philosopher who emphasized filial piety, ritual, and moral virtue in the Analects

cards.front: Which Chinese philosopher taught about filial piety and proper conduct in the Analects?

cards.back: Chinese philosopher traditionally credited as the founder of Taoism and author of the Tao Te Ching

cards.front: Which philosopher is credited as the founder of Taoism?

cards.back: German philosopher who co-wrote The Communist Manifesto and developed theories of class struggle and historical materialism

cards.front: Which philosopher co-wrote The Communist Manifesto with Friedrich Engels?

cards.back: English philosopher who developed utilitarianism and wrote On Liberty

cards.front: Which philosopher wrote On Liberty and is the leading figure of utilitarianism?

cards.back: Scottish philosopher and key figure of empiricism who argued knowledge comes from sensory experience

cards.front: Which Scottish philosopher was a leading figure of empiricism and skepticism?

cards.back: Danish philosopher considered the father of existentialism

cards.front: Which philosopher is considered the father of existentialism?

cards.back: French existentialist philosopher who wrote Being and Nothingness and argued "existence precedes essence"

cards.front: Which philosopher wrote Being and Nothingness and argued "existence precedes essence"?

cards.back: French existentialist philosopher and feminist who wrote The Second Sex

cards.front: Which philosopher wrote The Second Sex, a foundational text of modern feminism?

cards.back: Ancient Greek school teaching virtue, self-control, and acceptance of what one cannot change; founded by Zeno of Citium

cards.front: Which school of philosophy teaches virtue through self-control and acceptance of fate?

cards.back: Ancient Greek school teaching that pleasure (especially tranquility) is the highest good; founded by Epicurus

cards.front: Which school of philosophy teaches that pleasure and the absence of pain are the highest good?

cards.back: Philosophical movement emphasizing individual freedom, choice, and the search for meaning in an indifferent universe

cards.front: Which philosophical movement holds that existence precedes essence and emphasizes individual freedom?

cards.back: Ethical theory that the best action is the one producing the greatest good for the greatest number; developed by Jeremy Bentham and John Stuart Mill

cards.front: Which ethical theory judges actions by whether they produce the greatest good for the greatest number?

cards.back: Philosophical view that reason, rather than sensory experience, is the primary source of knowledge; associated with Descartes, Spinoza, and Leibniz

cards.front: Which philosophical view holds that reason is the primary source of knowledge?

cards.back: Philosophical view that knowledge comes primarily from sensory experience; associated with Locke, Hume, and Berkeley

cards.front: Which philosophical view holds that knowledge comes primarily from sensory experience?

cards.back: Philosophical view that life lacks objective meaning, purpose, or intrinsic value; often associated with Nietzsche

cards.front: Which philosophical view holds that life lacks objective meaning or purpose?

cards.back: American philosophical tradition that evaluates ideas by their practical consequences; developed by William James and Charles Sanders Peirce

cards.front: Which American philosophical tradition judges ideas by their practical consequences?
Social Contract theory — what is it?
cards.back: Theory that people agree to give up some freedoms to a government in exchange for protection of their remaining rights; associated with Hobbes, Locke, and Rousseau

cards.front: Who wrote Critique of Pure Reason?
Who wrote Meditations on First Philosophy?
What is Plato's Allegory of the Cave?
cards.back: A thought experiment in The Republic illustrating how people mistake shadows for reality until they see the true world outside the cave
A form of inquiry using a series of questions to stimulate critical thinking and draw out underlying assumptions
A moral principle stating you should act only according to rules you could will to be universal laws for everyone

cards.front: "Cogito, ergo sum" — what does it mean and who said it?
cards.back: "I think, therefore I am" — René Descartes, establishing that the act of thinking proves one's existence
